source.title: Adaptive laboratory evolution of Corynebacterium glutamicum towards higher growth rates on glucose minimal medium

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to improve the growth rate of Corynebacterium glutamicum on glucose minimal medium using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE). It compares the wild-type ATCC 13032 and its prophage-free variant MB001 to assess whether prophage removal affects genomic stability and evolvability. The goal is to identify key mutations that enhance fitness and to understand the underlying mechanisms.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution of Corynebacterium glutamicum towards higher growth rates on glucose minimal medium
section_gists.gist: The abstract and introduction state the problem: improving growth of C. glutamicum on glucose minimal medium. They highlight the importance of ALE combined with NGS for microbial adaptation and metabolic engineering. The study compares ATCC 13032 and its prophage-free variant MB001 to test if prophage removal affects genomic stability and evolvability. Initial hypothesis: prophage elements may influence mutation frequency and genomic rearrangements. The paper notes that MB001 has a slightly higher growth rate and a competitive advantage over the wild type. The introduction also mentions that ALE parameters (batch vs. continuous, passage size) impact outcomes.
process_signals: MB001 showed a slight but not significant growth rate increase over wild type in previous studies.
Competitive growth experiment showed MB001 has a significant advantage after three serial transfers.
The study questions whether lack of prophages affects genomic stability or evolvability.
methods: Competitive growth experiment with fluorescently labeled strains
metrics: Competitive fitness (population fraction over serial transfers)
failures_or_limits: Not explicitly stated in this section.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive evolution experiment
section_gists.gist: The ALE experiment design: six independent cell lines each for ATCC 13032 and MB001 were evolved in 48-well plates with CGXII minimal medium + 2% glucose at 30°C. Serial transfers (90 times) from stationary phase to fresh medium, totaling ~630 generations. Samples were taken at intervals for growth rate measurement and genome sequencing. A second reproduction experiment was performed to confirm the early fitness leap. Growth rates of 24 isolates per strain were measured at various time points. A significant fitness increase (~20%) was observed after ~100 generations for both strains. Top clones UBw (from ATCC 13032) and UBm (from MB001) showed ~26% increased growth rate. The box plot shows high diversity at 100 generations, narrowing later.
process_signals: Fitness leap of ~20% after ~100 generations in all six cell lines.
High diversity in populations at 100 generations, narrowing after 140 generations.
Reproduction experiment confirmed fitness leap after ~80 generations.
No difference in final OD600 between evolved and parental strains.
Evolved strains showed improved osmotic stress tolerance but similar heat and UV sensitivity.
methods: Growth rate measurement using BioLector microcultivation system
Isolation of 24 clones per strain at each time point for growth rate measurement
failures_or_limits: Not explicitly stated in this section.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Competitive growth experiment confirmed MB001's advantage over wild type. ALE experiment: both strains showed similar adaptation, with growth rate increase from ~0.51-0.53 to ~0.62-0.65 h^-1 after 100 generations. Top clones UBw and UBm had growth rates of 0.64 and 0.67 h^-1, respectively. Genome sequencing of two cell lines per strain at 100, 140, 540, 630 generations revealed 70 potentially causative SNPs and several transposon insertions. Key mutated genes: pyk (pyruvate kinase), fruK (1-phosphofructokinase), corA (Mg2+ transporter), hmuV, soxA, tnp, and others. No significant differences in mutation frequency or transposon activity between strains. Mutations in pyk and fruK were frequent and occurred independently in multiple lines.
process_signals: MB001 competitive advantage confirmed.
Both strains adapted similarly; no significant difference in genomic stability.
Key mutations in pyk, fruK, corA identified as potentially causative.
Number of SNPs increased over time, but no difference between strains.
Transposon insertions observed in both strains.
failures_or_limits: Not all mutations were validated; only selected ones reintroduced.
Some mutations may be neutral or hitchhiking.

section_gists.heading: generations
section_gists.gist: Mutation frequencies over time: mutations in pyk, fruK, corA, and other genes increased in frequency over generations, indicating positive selection. Different mutations in the same gene often occurred in independent lines. Trade-offs: UBw showed impaired growth on gluconate, ribose, acetate, and no growth on lactate (due to transposon insertion in lactate permease). UBm showed competitive or improved growth on all tested carbon sources and osmotic stress. Reintroduced pyk mutations (T12A, A20V) decreased PK activity and improved growth on glucose but impaired gluconate growth. fruK mutations had variable effects: T6I impaired fructose growth, R71L improved it; both improved gluconate growth. corA mutations did not improve glucose growth but helped under Mg2+ limitation.
process_signals: Mutation frequencies increased over time, indicating selection.
Parallel evolution: same genes mutated in independent lines.
Trade-offs observed: UBw lost ability to use some carbon sources; UBm did not.
pyk mutations decreased PK activity but increased growth rate (except A271T).
fruK mutations had divergent effects on fructose vs. gluconate.
corA mutations beneficial only under low Mg2+.
methods: Growth assays on different carbon sources
Reintroduction of single mutations into wild type
metrics: Growth rate on various carbon sources
failures_or_limits: UBw lost lactate utilization due to transposon insertion.
pyk A271T did not decrease PK activity but still improved growth slightly.
corA mutations alone did not improve glucose growth; may be beneficial only under specific conditions.
Not all mutations were tested in combination.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The study achieved ~20% fitness increase in ~100 generations, faster than E. coli long-term experiment. Key mutations in pyk, fruK, corA were causative. pyk mutations likely reduce PK activity, increasing PEP pool and driving PTS, but glucose uptake was not increased in low-activity mutants; only A271T increased uptake. fruK mutations may affect fructose metabolism and PTS regulation. corA mutations improve Mg2+ uptake under limitation. UBm showed no significant trade-offs, making it a promising chassis. Prophage removal did not affect evolvability; CGP3 prophage is silenced by CgpS. Limitations: only two cell lines sequenced per strain; not all mutations validated; trade-offs not fully explored; mechanisms of fruK and corA not fully elucidated.
process_signals: Fitness increase faster than in E. coli long-term experiment.
pyk mutations decrease PK activity, but mechanism of growth improvement may not be solely via increased PTS flux.
fruK mutations may affect multiple pathways.
corA mutations are conditionally beneficial.
Prophage elements stably maintained; no impact on evolvability.
UBm is a superior chassis due to lack of trade-offs.
failures_or_limits: Only two cell lines per strain sequenced; may miss rare mutations.
Not all mutations were reconstructed; some may be synergistic.
Mechanism of fruK and corA mutations not fully understood.
ALE in batch culture may select for stationary phase survival traits.

section_gists.heading: Material and Methods
section_gists.gist: Strains: ATCC 13032 and MB001. Growth conditions: BHI complex medium or CGXII minimal medium with 2% glucose, 30°C. ALE: 48-well FlowerPlates, 900 rpm, 30°C, serial transfer every 48-72 h (1:40 dilution), 90 transfers (~630 generations). First 20 transfers alternated with iron-depleted medium (no effect, discontinued). Glycerol stocks stored. Growth characterization: BioLector, 48-well plates, 750 µl CGXII, 1200 rpm, start OD600=1. Genome sequencing: Illumina MiSeq, 2x150 bp, TruSeq DNA PCR-free library prep. Mutation reintroduction: two-step homologous recombination. Barcoding: 20 bp unique sequences integrated into genome.
process_signals: Iron depletion alternation had no effect and was discontinued.
Barcoding allowed strain tracking and contamination detection.
Glycerol stocks enabled re-inoculation and analysis.
methods: ALE in microtiter plates with serial transfer
Genome sequencing and analysis with CLC Genomics Workbench
Recombinant DNA work: Gibson assembly, two-step homologous recombination
failures_or_limits: Iron depletion step was abandoned as it showed no effect.
Only two cell lines per strain were sequenced for most time points.
ALE conditions (batch, stationary phase transfer) may impose specific selection pressures.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The prophage-free strain MB001 has a slight growth advantage and may be a more stable chassis, but its evolvability is comparable to wild type.
Key mutations in pyk, fruK, and corA are the main drivers of fitness improvement on glucose minimal medium.
pyk mutations reduce PK activity, potentially increasing PEP pool and PTS flux, but the mechanism may be more complex.
fruK mutations may alter fructose metabolism and affect PTS regulation, leading to improved glucose utilization.
corA mutations improve Mg2+ uptake, which is beneficial under the high-salt conditions of CGXII medium.
Trade-offs are strain-specific: UBw accumulated detrimental mutations, while UBm did not, possibly due to different genetic backgrounds or stochastic effects.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did pyk A271T not reduce PK activity but still improve growth?
What is the exact mechanism by which fruK mutations improve glucose growth?
Are corA mutations beneficial only under Mg2+ limitation, or do they have other effects?
Why did UBm avoid trade-offs while UBw did not? Is it due to prophage absence or chance?
Would other mutations (e.g., in hmuV, soxA) contribute to fitness if tested in combination?
How would the strains perform in continuous culture or different selection regimes?
Are the identified mutations sufficient to explain the full fitness increase, or are there epistatic interactions?

events.summary: The study aims to improve growth rate of C. glutamicum on glucose minimal medium and compare wild-type ATCC 13032 with prophage-free MB001.
Competitive growth experiment showed MB001 has a significant advantage over wild type after three serial transfers.
ALE experiment design: six independent cell lines each for ATCC 13032 and MB001, repetitive batch cultivation in 48-well plates, serial transfer 90 times, ~630 generations.
Fitness leap of ~20% observed after ~100 generations for both strains.
No significant differences in mutation frequency or transposon activity between ATCC 13032 and MB001.
Reintegration of pyk and fruK mutations increased glucose consumption rate and ptsG expression.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers the abstract, introduction, and early results, providing key events: problem statement, hypothesis, competitive growth experiment, ALE design, fitness leap observation, genome sequencing results, and validation of mutations.

events.summary: Sequencing revealed 70 potentially causative SNPs and transposon insertions, with no significant difference in mutation frequency between strains.
pyk gene was frequently mutated with five different mutations appearing early and showing competitive dynamics.
fruK gene was identified as another key target with two SNPs and a transposon insertion, one occurring independently in two lines.
corA mutations appeared only at later stages, suggesting epistatic interactions supporting evolution towards higher growth rates.
Combination of pyk A271T and fruK R71L led to even higher growth rate, demonstrating synergistic effect.
pyk mutations T12A and A20V decreased PK activity, but A271T did not, yet A271T had strongest growth impact.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of mutations, their frequencies, and experimental validation of key mutations' effects on growth and enzyme activity.

events.summary: Investigated effects of key mutations on utilization of different carbon sources.
methods_or_tools: growth assays on fructose, gluconate, sucrose

events.summary: fruK T6I caused strong growth defect on fructose; R71L slightly improved fitness.
pyk A20V and T12A strongly impeded growth on gluconate; A271T not affected.
All fruK mutations caused significant improvement on gluconate.
Reintroduced pyk mutations T12A and A20V decreased PK activity; A271T did not significantly alter PK activity.
No significant differences in adaptive behavior, mutation frequency or transposon activity between wild type and prophage-free MB001.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of mutation effects on carbon source utilization, PK activity, glucose uptake, and comparative fitness of evolved strains, directly supporting the paper's research evolution.

events.summary: Analysis of fruK mutations T6I and R71L effects on growth and fructose metabolism
methods_or_tools: growth experiments on fructose minimal medium

events.summary: Hypothesis that fruK deletion increases glucose uptake via hexose phosphate pool changes relieving SugR repression
Observation of increased ptsG expression and glucose uptake in fruK R71L mutant
Hypothesis that reduced CorA activity increases metabolic flux on glucose
Uncharacterized epistatic interactions likely contribute to fitness

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis and experimental validation of fruK and corA mutations, including hypotheses on mechanisms and limitations regarding epistasis.

events.summary: Hypothesized that pyk and fruK mutations increase glucose uptake by elevating PEP pool or hexose phosphates, relieving SugR repression.
ALE experiment designed with six biological replicates per strain, serial transfers every 48-72 h, 90 transfers total.
First 20 transfers alternated with iron-depleted medium to test prophage induction, but discontinued due to no effect.
Genome sequencing performed on two cell lines per strain at 100, 140, 540, 630 generations, and one line at 50 and 80 generations from repeated ALE.
Growth rates calculated from BioLector backscatter data using exponential fit after background subtraction.
Glucose uptake rate determined from supernatant concentrations and growth rate, using correlation factor 0.25 g dry weight per L per OD600.
numbers_or_metrics: 0.25 g dry weight per L per OD600

evidence_bank.quote: In this work, we performed a comparative adaptive laboratory evolution experiment of the important biotechnological platform strain Corynebacterium glutamicum ATCC 13032 and its prophage-free variant MB001 towards improved growth rates on glucose minimal medium.
already after three serial transfers a significant competitive advantage for strain MB001 (for both reporter strains, vice versa) was observed
Overall, six independent cell lines for each strain were evolved by repetitive batch cultivation using 48-well plates incubated at 30°C
serially transferred (90 times) from the stationary phase into fresh medium
a significant increase in fitness was observed for all six cell lines after approx. 100 generations
no significant differences, neither in the mutation frequency nor in transposon activity, were observed between ATCC 13032 and MB001
The reintegration of selected pyk and fruK mutations resulted in an increased glucose consumption rate and ptsG expression causative for the accelerated growth on glucose minimal medium
no significant differences, neither in the mutation frequency nor in transposon activity, were observed between ATCC 13032 and MB001
the pyk gene, encoding a pyruvate kinase, was one of the most frequently affected genes with five different mutations
these pyk mutations already appeared in an early stage of the experiment (after 100 & 140 generations) in low frequencies and displayed a competitive dynamic progression within the population
analysis of the sequencing data revealed the phosphofructokinase gene fruK as another important key target
one of the two fruK SNPs (T6I) occurred independently at different time points in the two prophage-free cell lines
only observed at later stages in the ALE experiment suggesting epistatic interactions of corA mutations supporting the evolution towards higher growth rates on glucose
The combination of pyk A271T and fruK R71L (as identified in strain UBm) led to an even higher growth rate on glucose demonstrating the synergistic effect of these key targets
in vitro enzyme assays revealed a significantly decreased pyruvate kinase activity for the single mutants T12A and A20V
no difference in pyruvate kinase activity was measured for A271T
this mutant showed the strongest impact on growth
we investigated the effects of key mutations on the utilization of different carbon sources, including fructose, gluconate and in case of fruK also sucrose.
Whereas the fruK T6I mutation led to a strong growth defect on fructose, R71L slightly improved the fitness.
In the pyk mutants, A20V and T12A, growth on gluconate was strongly impeded, but the A271T mutant was not affected.
all fruK mutations caused a significant improvement when gluconate served as sole carbon source
we showed that two of the mutations (T12A, A20V), in fact, led to a decrease in PK activity, whereas the third mutation A271T did not significantly alter the PK activity in the in vitro assay

we observed no significant differences in the adaptive behavior, mutation frequency or transposon activity of C. glutamicum ATCC 13032 wild type and its prophage-free derivative MB001 in our comparative ALE experiment
Growth experiments on fructose minimal medium revealed a negative impact of the T6I mutation (Figure S7) comparable to a fruK deletion strain
R71L mutants acquired benefits by surpassing slightly the growth of the parental strain
The authors suggested that fruK deletion results in an increased pool of F1P or in other hexose phosphates relieving SugR repression of ptsG
an increased expression of ptsG and a higher glucose uptake rate was observed for the fruK R71L mutant
these findings suggest that reduced CorA activity may also act by increasing metabolic flux on glucose, thereby conferring a competitive advantage
it is likely to assume that so far uncharacterized epistatic interactions between the genomic variants significantly contribute to an improved fitness of the evolved populations

We hypothesize that mutations in pyk and fruK both contribute to an elevated glucose uptake by increasing the PEP pool (pyk mutations) or by an accumulation of hexose phosphates (fruK) relieving SugR repression.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Improve growth rate of C. glutamicum on glucose minimal medium via ALE and compare evolvability of wild-type ATCC 13032 and prophage-free MB001.

canonical_events.summary: Goal: improve growth rate of C. glutamicum on glucose minimal medium; compare wild-type ATCC 13032 and prophage-free MB001.
Competitive growth experiment: MB001 shows significant advantage over wild type after three serial transfers.
ALE design: six independent cell lines per strain, repetitive batch cultivation in 48-well plates, 90 serial transfers (~630 generations).
First 20 transfers alternated with iron-depleted medium to test prophage induction; discontinued due to no effect.
Fitness leap of ~20% observed after ~100 generations for both strains; growth rates increase from ~0.51-0.53 to ~0.62-0.65 h⁻¹.

Genome sequencing of two cell lines per strain at 100, 140, 540, 630 generations reveals 70 potentially causative SNPs and transposon insertions; no significant difference in mutation frequency between strains.
pyk gene frequently mutated (five different mutations) appearing early and showing competitive dynamics; fruK identified as another key target with two SNPs and a transposon insertion.
corA mutations appear only at later stages, suggesting epistatic interactions supporting evolution towards higher growth rates.
Reintroduction of pyk and fruK mutations increases glucose consumption rate and ptsG expression; combination of pyk A271T and fruK R71L shows synergistic effect on growth rate.
pyk mutations T12A and A20V decrease PK activity, but A271T does not; A271T has strongest growth impact.
fruK T6I causes strong growth defect on fructose; R71L slightly improves fitness; all fruK mutations improve growth on gluconate.
pyk A20V and T12A strongly impede growth on gluconate; A271T not affected.
No significant differences in adaptive behavior, mutation frequency or transposon activity between wild type and prophage-free MB001.
Uncharacterized epistatic interactions likely contribute to fitness; only two cell lines per strain sequenced; not all mutations validated; trade-offs not fully explored.

edges.reason: Competitive growth experiment provides initial evidence of MB001 advantage, motivating comparative ALE.
ALE design is set up to compare the two strains under identical conditions.
Iron depletion step was part of the initial ALE protocol but later discontinued.
ALE experiment leads to observation of fitness leap after ~100 generations.
Fitness leap prompts genome sequencing to identify causative mutations.
Sequencing reveals pyk and fruK as key mutated genes.
Sequencing also reveals corA mutations appearing later.
Identification of pyk and fruK mutations leads to reintroduction experiments to validate their effects.
Reintroduction experiments include detailed enzyme assays to characterize pyk mutations.
Reintroduction experiments also characterize fruK mutations on different carbon sources.
Analysis of pyk mutations on gluconate reveals trade-offs.
Comparison of mutation frequencies confirms no significant difference between strains.
Despite similar evolvability, limitations in the study are acknowledged.

must_cover_points: Comparative ALE of ATCC 13032 and MB001
Identification of pyk, fruK, and corA as key mutated genes
Reintroduction of mutations validates growth improvement
pyk A271T does not reduce PK activity but has strongest growth impact
fruK mutations have divergent effects on fructose and gluconate
No significant difference in evolvability between strains
